content: Which from following reactions performs highest + ve work at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
explanation:
Work done in chemical reaction:

$\mathrm{W}=-\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}} \mathrm{RT}$

(A) $\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=2-2=0$

(B) $\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=2-4=-2$.

(C) $\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=2-\frac{7}{2}=-\frac{3}{2}$

(D) $\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}=4-9=-5$

For highest +ve work done, $\Delta \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{g}}$ should be highly negative.

Hence, the reaction in option (D) will perform the highest positive work at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

content: Calculate standard Gibbs energy for a certain reaction if $\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}$ respectively are $219 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $-20 \mathrm{JK}^{-1}$.
Calculate the change in internal energy of the system if 15 kJ of work done on the surrounding and system releases 10 kJ of heat in a particular reaction.
explanation:
Since work is done by the system on the surroundings, W is negative.

As the system releases heat, Q is also is also negative.

$$ \therefore \quad \Delta \mathrm{U}=\mathrm{Q}+\mathrm{W}=(-10) \mathrm{kJ}+(-15 \mathrm{~kJ})=-25 \mathrm{~kJ} $$

content: For a certain reaction, $\Delta \mathrm{H}^{\circ}=219 \mathrm{~kJ}$ and $\Delta \mathrm{S}^{\circ}=-21 \mathrm{JK}^{-1}$. Find the value of $\Delta \mathrm{G}^{\circ}$.

Calculate the work done in following reaction.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_{4(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{HCl}_{(\mathrm{g})} \longrightarrow \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{g})} \text { at } 27^{\circ} \mathrm{C} . \\ & \left(\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right) \end{aligned} $$
explanation:
Step 1: Use the work done formula for gases

The work done by a gas in a reaction is given by: $$ W = -\Delta n_g RT $$ where:
$W$ = work done,
$\Delta n_g$ = change in number of moles of gas,
$R$ = gas constant,
$T$ = temperature in Kelvin.

Step 2: Find $\Delta n_g$ (change in moles of gas)

Count moles of gas on both sides:
On left: $\mathrm{C}_2\mathrm{H}_4(\mathrm{g})$ (1 mole) + $\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{g})$ (1 mole) = 2 moles.
On right: $\mathrm{C}_2\mathrm{H}_5\mathrm{Cl}(\mathrm{g})$ (1 mole).
So, $\Delta n_g = $ moles of gas (products) $-$ moles of gas (reactants) $= 1 - 2 = -1$.

Step 3: Substitute values

Given: $R = 8.314~\mathrm{J~K}^{-1}\mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ and $T = 27^\circ\mathrm{C} = 300~\mathrm{K}$.
Now plug in values:
$W = -(-1) \times 8.314 \times 300$

Step 4: Calculate the result

$W = 1 \times 8.314 \times 300 = 2494.2~\mathrm{J}$

content: One mole of a perfect gas expands isothermally and reversibly from $10 \mathrm{dm}^3$ to $20 \mathrm{dm}^3$ at 300 K . Find $\Delta \mathrm{U}, \mathrm{q}$ and work done respectively in the process. $\left(\mathrm{R}=8.3 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
explanation:
Step 1: Recall the concept.

For an isothermal, reversible expansion of an ideal gas:

  • Internal energy change, $\Delta U = 0$ (because $U$ depends only on temperature for an ideal gas, and $T$ is constant).

  • Work done by the gas:

$$ w = -nRT \ln \frac{V_f}{V_i} $$

(The negative sign is taken because work done by the system is taken as negative in thermodynamics convention of NCERT).

  • From the First Law:

$$ \Delta U = q + w $$

Since $\Delta U = 0$, therefore $q = -w$.

Step 2: Known values

  • $n = 1 \ \text{mol}$

  • $R = 8.3 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}\text{K}^{-1}$

  • $T = 300 \ \text{K}$

  • $V_i = 10 \ \text{dm}^3$

  • $V_f = 20 \ \text{dm}^3$

Step 3: Calculate work done

$$ w = -nRT \ln \frac{V_f}{V_i} $$

$$ w = -(1)(8.3 \times 10^{-3})(300) \ln \frac{20}{10} $$

$$ w = -(2.49) \ln(2) $$

Since $\ln 2 = 0.693$,

$$ w = -(2.49)(0.693) $$

$$ w = -1.726 \ \text{kJ} $$

Step 4: Find $q$

Since $\Delta U = 0$,

$$ q = -w = +1.726 \ \text{kJ} $$

Step 5: Final Answer

  • $\Delta U = 0.0 \ \text{kJ}$

  • $q = +1.726 \ \text{kJ}$

  • $w = -1.726 \ \text{kJ}$

Correct Option: A ✅

content:
Calculate heat of formation of $\mathrm{SO}_2$ from following equations.

$$ \begin{aligned} & \mathrm{S}+\frac{3}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{SO}_3, \Delta \mathrm{H}=-2 \mathrm{xkJ} \\ & \mathrm{SO}_2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{SO}_3, \Delta \mathrm{H}=-\mathrm{ykJ} \end{aligned} $$
explanation: $$ \text { On rearranging the second reaction, } $$

content: Which of the following symbols represents heat of reaction at constant pressure?
explanation:
$$ \begin{aligned} & \Delta U=q_p-P \Delta V \\ & q_p=\Delta U+P \Delta V \end{aligned} $$

Step 1: $\Delta U$ means change in internal energy. $q_p$ means heat given at constant pressure. $P\Delta V$ is pressure times change in volume.

Step 2: At constant pressure, the heat ($q_p$) is equal to the change in enthalpy.

$$ \Delta H=\Delta U+P \Delta V $$

So, the symbol for heat of reaction at constant pressure is $\Delta H$ (enthalpy change).

content: The entropy of vaporisation of benzene is $85 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. When 117 g of benzene vaporises at its boiling point, what is entropy change of surrounding if process is at equilibrium?
explanation:
$\Delta_{\text {vap }} \mathrm{H}$ of benzene for 1 mole $=85 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$

117 g of $\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_6=\frac{117 \mathrm{~g}}{78 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}}=1.5$ moles

$\therefore \quad \Delta_{\text {vap }} \mathrm{H}$ for 117 g of $\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_6=-85 \times 1.5 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$

content: Which from following terms is explained by first law of thermodynamics?
explanation:
First law of thermodynamics is essentially the law of conservation of energy. It states that energy can neither be created nor destroyed, only transformed from one form to another.

– Entropy (Option A) is addressed by the second law.

– Free energy (Option B, Gibbs or Helmholtz) involves both first and second laws but is not itself the first law.

– Enthalpy (Option D) is a state function (heat content at constant pressure), not the law itself.

Answer: Option C – Conservation of energy.

content:
What is the change in internal energy for $2 \mathrm{CO}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?

( $\mathrm{R}=8.314 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~K}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}, \Delta \mathrm{H}=-560 \mathrm{~kJ}$ )
explanation:
Step 1: Write the relation between enthalpy change and internal energy change:

$$\Delta H = \Delta U + \Delta n\,R\,T$$

Step 2: Identify each term for this reaction:

ΔH = –560 kJ

R = 8.314 J K–1 mol–1

T = 298 K

Δn = 1

Step 3: Substitute the values and solve for ΔU:

$$ \begin{aligned} & \Delta H=\Delta U+\Delta n R T \\ & -560=\Delta U+(1 \times 8.314 \times 298) \\ \therefore \quad & \Delta U=-557.5 \mathrm{~kJ} \end{aligned} $$
